habitat: Native pine woodland, plantation edges, hill slopes
lifecycle: Jan-Mar: Dormant. Apr-May: new needles form. May-Jun: male and female flowers. Jun-Oct: cones develop. Oct-Dec: cones ripen and release seeds.
identification: Tall evergreen conifer; orange-brown upper bark; long blue-green needles in pairs; oval woody cones on short stalks.
notes: Historically important for timber and shipbuilding; resin used as antiseptic; needles rich in vitamin C (pine needle tea); symbol of the Caledonian Forest and Scottish uplands.

habitat: Well-drained slopes, woodland edges, open ground
lifecycle: Jan-Mar: bare; catkins formed. Apr-May: leaves emerge, male catkins shed pollen. Jun-Aug: seeds develop. Sep-Oct: leaves yellow and fall. Nov-Dec: dormant.
identification: White peeling bark with dark fissures; drooping twigs; triangular toothed leaves; hanging catkins in spring.
notes: Pioneer tree that colonises open ground; associated with many fungi (including birch boletes and fly agaric); birch sap historically tapped for a sweet drink; twigs used as brooms and besoms in folklore.

habitat: Damp ground, heath edges, upland woodland
lifecycle: Similar to Silver Birch: spring leafing and catkins in Apr-May; seed ripening in late summer; leaf fall in autumn; winter dormant.
identification: More upright than Silver Birch; young shoots hairy; oval leaves with more rounded base; prefers wetter soils.
notes: Important in upland and bog-edge habitats; stabilises wet soils; supports a wide range of insects, lichens and fungi.

habitat: Woodland edges, rocky slopes, open hillside
lifecycle: Mar-Apr: buds burst. May-Jun: white flower clusters. Jul-Sep: red berries ripen. Oct: leaves turn red/orange and fall. Nov-Feb: berries often persist then are eaten by birds.
identification: Slender tree with pinnate leaves; flat-topped clusters of white flowers; bright red berry clusters in late summer.
notes: Strong protective tree in Scottish folklore; rowan sprigs used to ward off evil and witches; berries eaten by birds and can be made into jelly (after cooking).

lifecycle: Feb-Mar: purple male catkins and small female cones. Apr-May: leaves emerge. Jun-Aug: seeds develop. Sep-Oct: seeds shed; leaves turn dull and fall. Winter: woody cones remain.
identification: Dark bark; rounded, often notched leaves; clusters of small cone-like fruits; roots often in waterlogged soils.
notes: Nitrogen-fixing tree improving soils; wood resists rot under water, once used for foundations and clog soles; associated with water spirits in folklore.

lifecycle: Jan-Mar: yellow catkins release pollen. Mar-Apr: red female flowers tiny on stems. Aug-Oct: nuts ripen. Oct-Nov: leaves yellow and fall. Winter: dormant.
identification: Multi-stemmed shrub with rounded, slightly hairy leaves; long yellow catkins in late winter; clusters of brown nuts in leafy husks.
notes: Hazel nuts are edible and nutritious; coppiced hazel used for hurdles and thatching; strongly associated with wisdom and inspiration in Celtic lore.

lifecycle: Mar-Apr: white blossom before or with leaves. May-Jul: leaves fully out; fruits develop. Jul-Aug: cherries ripen. Sep-Oct: leaves turn red/orange and fall. Winter: dormant.
identification: Smooth grey-brown bark often with horizontal lines; clusters of white five-petalled flowers; red cherries on stalks.
notes: Fruits eaten by birds and people (when found); wood prized for furniture; blossom a classic sign of spring.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves and long drooping flower racemes appear. Jun-Jul: small black cherries form. Aug-Sep: fruits ripen; leaves yellow and fall. Winter dormant.
identification: Long fragrant tassels of white flowers; bitter black cherries; leaves slightly elongated with finely toothed edges.
notes: Very attractive to insects when in blossom; bitter fruits used in some traditional recipes; associated with damp, cool woods.

lifecycle: Feb-Mar: catkins produced. Apr-May: leaves open. Jun-Aug: seeds ripen and disperse. Sep-Oct: leaves turn golden and fall. Winter: dormant.
identification: Roundish leaves with long flattened stalks that cause constant trembling; light bark; forms small clonal groves.
notes: Leaves ‘tremble’ in the slightest breeze; important for specialised insects; wood used for lightweight items; features in folklore as a restless, whispering tree.

lifecycle: Evergreen. Apr-Jun: small white flowers. Aug-Dec: red berries develop and ripen; berries persist into winter, especially on female plants.
identification: Evergreen with glossy spiny leaves (upper parts of older trees may be less spiny); bright red berries on female trees.
notes: Traditional winter decoration; berries are poisonous to humans but vital winter food for birds; sacred tree in folklore symbolising protection and endurance.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves and white blossom. Jun-Aug: haws (red fruits) develop. Sep-Oct: fruits ripen; leaves yellow and fall. Winter: bare.
identification: Dense thorny branches; deeply lobed leaves; masses of white, sometimes pinkish blossom; red haws in autumn.
notes: Important hedgerow species providing nectar, berries and shelter; in folklore associated with May Day and fairy sites; flowers once used in remedies for the heart.

lifecycle: May-Jun: flat clusters of creamy flowers. Jul-Sep: black berries ripen. Oct-Nov: leaves yellow and fall. Winter: bare.
identification: Rough grey bark; compound leaves; flat-topped umbels of creamy fragrant flowers; drooping clusters of black berries.
notes: Flowers used for cordial and wine; berries cooked for syrups and jams (raw berries can upset the stomach); strong folklore associations with protection and household spirits.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves and catkins appear. Jun-Oct: acorns develop and ripen. Oct-Nov: leaf colour change and fall. Winter: dormant.
identification: Large broadleaf with lobed leaves lacking long stalks; acorns sit directly on twigs (‘sessile’).
notes: Supports huge numbers of insects, lichens and fungi; wood historically valued for building and barrels; revered in Celtic tradition.

lifecycle: Evergreen; growth flush in spring; cones produced and ripen late summer to autumn.
identification: Tall conifer with drooping branchlets; sharp stiff needles; papery hanging cones.
notes: Main commercial forestry species in much of Scotland; provides shelter but relatively low native biodiversity compared to Scots pine.

lifecycle: Evergreen; cones develop over summer and ripen in autumn.
identification: Conical crown; long hanging cylindrical cones; dark green needles on all sides of twig.
notes: Traditional ‘Christmas tree’; plantations may occur around Bennachie as part of mixed conifer forestry.

lifecycle: Evergreen; cones maturing late summer-autumn.
identification: Very tall conifer; soft flat needles; distinctive cones with three-pointed bracts ‘mouse tails’ between scales.
notes: Valuable timber tree; cones and bark have strong resinous scent; not native but used in Scottish forestry.

lifecycle: Mar-Apr: bright green needles appear. Apr-May: pinkish female flowers (larch roses). Sep-Oct: needles turn golden and fall; cones remain on branches over winter.
identification: Deciduous conifer with soft needles in clusters; small upright cones; golden autumn colour.
notes: Deciduous conifer giving striking spring and autumn colour; timber used for outdoor structures; hybrid larch may also be present.

lifecycle: Jan-Mar: evergreen shoots. Apr-Jun: buds form. Jul-Sep: peak flowering. Sep-Nov: seed set. Nov-Dec: foliage persists.
identification: Low, dense shrub; tiny scale-like leaves pressed to stems; masses of small purple-pink bell-like flowers on spikes.
notes: Dominant plant of Scottish heather moorland; used traditionally for bedding, thatch and dye; heather honey prized; white heather considered lucky.

lifecycle: Apr-May: shoots extend. Jun-Aug: bell-shaped flowers. Sep-Oct: seed capsules. Winter: evergreen.
identification: Woody shrub with needle-like leaves in whorls of three; larger purple-pink bell-shaped flowers than Calluna.
notes: Important nectar source for bees and insects; contributes to the purple late-summer colour of upland heaths.

habitat: Wet heath, boggy ground, peaty hollows
lifecycle: May-Jun: growth and bud formation. Jun-Aug: clusters of drooping pink bells. Sep-Oct: seed capsules. Winter: evergreen.
identification: Soft grey-green leaves in whorls of four (cross-like); drooping clusters of pale pink bell-flowers.
notes: Indicator of wetter heaths and bogs; valued by insects; contributes to diversity of heathland colour and structure.

habitat: Pine and birch woodland, heath and moor edges
lifecycle: Mar-Apr: leaves emerge. Apr-Jun: small greenish-pink flowers. Jun-Aug: blue-black berries ripen. Sep-Oct: leaves redden and fall. Winter: stems bare.
identification: Low shrub with angular green stems; small oval leaves; dark blue berries that stain fingers and lips.
notes: Edible berries eaten fresh or in pies and jams; important food for grouse and other wildlife; known as ‘blaeberries’ in Scotland.

habitat: Dry pine woodland, heaths, rocky slopes
lifecycle: Evergreen. Apr-Jun: white-pink bell flowers. Jul-Sep: bright red berries. Autumn-winter: berries may persist.
identification: Low mat-forming shrub with leathery evergreen leaves; clusters of small bell-flowers; bright red berries.
notes: Berries edible when cooked, used in jams and sauces; important wildlife food; common in boreal and upland pinewoods.

lifecycle: Evergreen. Apr-Jun: small purplish flowers. Jul-Sep: shiny black berries. Berries may persist into winter.
identification: Very low mat-forming shrub with narrow needle-like leaves; small inconspicuous flowers; black berries along stems.
notes: Berries eaten by birds and sometimes people (rather bland); typical of high and exposed moorland; helps stabilise thin soils.

lifecycle: Evergreen. Apr-Jun: tiny flowers. Cones (‘berries’) take 2-3 years to ripen, usually blue-black by autumn of second/third year.
identification: Spiny needle-like leaves in whorls of three; blue-black berry-like cones when ripe; forms low bushes or small trees.
notes: Berries used to flavour gin and game dishes; important for specialised insects and fungi; a declining but iconic Scottish species.

lifecycle: Evergreen. Feb-Jun: main flowering period with bright yellow flowers. Some flowers may appear almost year-round. Seed pods ripen and pop in summer.
identification: Very spiny shrub with dark green needle-like spines; coconut-scented yellow pea-flowers.
notes: Provides dense shelter and nesting for birds; flowers once used for dye and as a coffee substitute; features strongly in upland scenery.

habitat: Heathland margins, dry banks, open woodland edges
lifecycle: Apr-Jun: bright yellow flowers. Jun-Aug: seed pods developing and then bursting. Winter: bare green shoots.
identification: Erect green, ridged stems; bright yellow pea-flowers; black pods that twist and pop open in sun.
notes: Related to gorse but less spiny; stems once used for brooms; showy spring flowers.

lifecycle: Apr-Jun: leaves expand and flower buds form. Jun-Aug: white/pink flowers. Aug-Oct: blackberries ripen. Nov-Mar: canes persist, some leaves remain in mild winters.
identification: Arching prickly stems; white or pink flowers; clusters of juicy black berries.
notes: Blackberries are widely eaten and used in jams and desserts; excellent wildlife plant for nectar and fruit.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves emerge. May-Jun: small white flowers. Jul-Aug: red raspberries ripen. Sep-Oct: canes die back after fruiting, new canes grow.
identification: Tall, often thorny canes with pinnate leaves; white flowers; red aggregate fruits (raspberries).
notes: Edible raspberries highly valued; an important plant for both people and wildlife; forms thickets in light woodland and clearings.

habitat: Wet heaths, bog margins, peaty hollows
lifecycle: Mar-Apr: catkins appear before leaves. May-Jun: leaves emerge. Summer: aromatic foliage. Autumn: leaves fall.
identification: Low aromatic shrub with narrow leaves; brownish catkins; grows in clumps in wet peaty soils.
notes: Leaves strongly aromatic; historically used to flavour beer and repel insects; associated with bogs and moorland lore.

habitat: Shaded birch and mixed woodland floors
lifecycle: Jan-Mar: mostly dormant. Apr-Jun: leaves and white flowers appear. Jul-Aug: seed capsules form and eject seeds. Sep-Oct: leaves may persist. Nov-Dec: dies back.
identification: Delicate three-part leaves like small clover; white flowers with lilac veins; often forms patches in damp shade.
notes: Leaves have a sharp lemony flavour (contain oxalic acid) and were used as a cooling herb; sometimes called 'Fairy Bells'; associated with woodland fairy lore.

lifecycle: Feb-Mar: leaves emerge. Mar-May: white star-like flowers open in sunshine. Jun-Jul: plants die back to underground rhizomes for rest of year.
identification: Carpet-forming plant with deeply divided leaves and white (sometimes pink-tinged) flowers on single stems.
notes: An ancient woodland indicator; early spring flowering before canopy closes; sometimes called 'windflower'; mildly toxic if eaten.

lifecycle: Mar-Apr: leaves and early flowers. Apr-Jun: main flowering period. Summer: seed capsules. Autumn-winter: leaves may persist or die back.
identification: Heart-shaped leaves; unscented purple-violet flowers; short spur at back of flower.
notes: Important larval food plant for some fritillary butterflies; pretty spring woodland and grassland flower.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves appear. May-Sep: yellow flowers. Sep-Oct: seeds ripen then plant dies back.
identification: Low herb with slender stems; small yellow flowers usually with four petals; leaves with three or more leaflets.
notes: Roots historically used as an astringent remedy for diarrhoea and sore throats; common on upland paths and heaths.

habitat: Damp meadows, wet heaths, grassy clearings
lifecycle: Apr-Jun: basal rosettes. Jul-Oct: lilac-blue button-like flower heads. Oct-Nov: seeds; foliage dies back over winter.
identification: Rounded purple-blue flower heads on long stems; basal leaves spoon-shaped.
notes: Excellent nectar plant for butterflies; folklore says the Devil bit off its roots in envy of its healing powers.

habitat: Grassland, path edges, open sunny slopes
lifecycle: Apr-May: new growth. May-Sep: yellow and orange 'eggs and bacon' flowers. Late summer: seed pods develop.
identification: Low sprawling plant; trifoliate leaves; clusters of pea-flowers yellow to orange-red; seed pods resemble a bird’s foot.
notes: Important food plant for many butterfly and moth larvae; widely used in wildlife-friendly grassland mixes.

lifecycle: Apr-May: growth and bud formation. May-Aug: pale lilac-blue flowers. Autumn: dies back to creeping stems.
identification: Creeping stems with opposite hairy leaves; upright spikes of small pale blue flowers with darker lines.
notes: Formerly used as a tea and general tonic; pretty but modest wildflower of uplands and heaths.

lifecycle: Apr-Jun: flowers begin. Jun-Sep: small pale blue or white flowers. Autumn-winter: creeping stems persist low to ground.
identification: Very small opposite leaves; delicate pale blue flowers with darker lines; creeping habit.
notes: Common small speedwell; often overlooked; provides nectar to tiny insects.

lifecycle: Apr-May: rosettes appear. May-Jul: pink tubular flowers. Aug-Sep: seed capsules. Winter: dies back.
identification: Low semi-parasitic plant with ferny leaves; pink hooded flowers in short spikes.
notes: Semi-parasitic on grasses; folklore said it caused lice in livestock, hence the name; important for upland biodiversity.

lifecycle: Similar to Lousewort but flowering slightly later (Jun-Aug).
identification: Taller than Lousewort with more elongated spikes; crimson-pink tubular flowers; finer, deeply cut leaves.
notes: Another semi-parasitic species; valued in diverse wetland plant communities.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves appear. Jun-Oct: flat-topped clusters of white (sometimes pink) flowers. Autumn: seeds set; foliage may persist low into winter.
identification: Feathery aromatic leaves; stiff stems topped with clusters of small daisy-like flowers.
notes: Long history as medicinal herb for wounds and nosebleeds; associated with divination; excellent nectar plant.

lifecycle: Apr-May: leaves and buds. May-Aug: purple flower clusters. Late summer: seed set; foliage persists low to ground.
identification: Very low mat-forming herb; tiny aromatic leaves; clusters of purple-pink flowers.
notes: Strongly aromatic; used sparingly in cooking; much loved by bees; associated with fairy legends on hilltops.

lifecycle: Apr-May: shoots appear. May-Aug: deep blue or purple flowers. Late summer: fruits and seeds.
identification: Small plant with narrow leaves; little spike of pea-like flowers that can be blue, purple or pink.
notes: Attractive but easily overlooked; related species once used in folk medicine for chest complaints.

lifecycle: First year: rosette of leaves. Second year: May-Aug tall flower spike of purple bells. Autumn: seeds set; plant dies after seeding.
identification: Tall spike with hanging purple tubular flowers, spotted inside; coarse leaves in basal rosette.
notes: Source of heart medicine (digitalis); highly toxic if eaten; striking, iconic flower of woodland clearings and cut-over ground.

lifecycle: May-Jun: leaves and buds. Jun-Sep: nodding blue bell-flowers. Autumn: seed capsules; foliage dies back.
identification: Slender stems with delicate nodding blue bell-shaped flowers; small basal leaves.
notes: Sometimes called the ‘Scottish bluebell’; associated with fairies and lonely hillsides in folklore.

habitat: Deciduous woodland (more localised in NE Scotland)
lifecycle: Mar-Apr: leaves and buds. Apr-May: blue bell-shaped flowers on one-sided drooping spike. Jun: seeds; by July foliage dies back.
identification: Linear leaves; deep blue scented bells drooping to one side of stem.
notes: Symbol of ancient woodland; protected in law from commercial digging; carpets woods with blue in spring where abundant.

habitat: Damp meadows, stream edges, wet ditches
lifecycle: Apr-May: foliage appears. Jun-Aug: creamy flower plumes. Aug-Sep: seeds form. Winter: stems die back.
identification: Tall plant with creamy frothy flower heads; leaves with serrated leaflets, underside whitish.
notes: Strong sweet scent; traditional remedy for pain and fevers (source of salicylic compounds related to aspirin); used to flavour drinks.

lifecycle: Mar-Apr: leaves and buds. Apr-May: bright yellow buttercup-like flowers. Jun-Jul: seed heads. Late summer-winter: dies back.
identification: Shiny heart-shaped leaves; large golden-yellow flowers in clumps near water.
notes: One of the earliest wetland flowers; sometimes called 'kingcups'; mildly toxic if eaten raw.

habitat: Acid bogs and very wet moorland
lifecycle: May-Jun: leaves and stems. Jul-Aug: spikes of golden-yellow starry flowers. Late summer: orange-brown seed heads.
identification: Stiff upright stems with narrow leaves; golden starry flowers along upper stem; later orange-brown spikes.
notes: Associated with poor, wet, peaty soils; folklore linked it to brittle bones in grazing animals (hence 'ossifragum').

lifecycle: Apr-May: sticky leaf rosette forms. May-Jul: violet flowers on stalks. Summer: leaves continue trapping insects. Autumn: forms winter bud and dies back above ground.
identification: Rosette of pale green greasy leaves that curl around trapped insects; single purple flower on slender stalk.
notes: Carnivorous plant that digests small insects; leaves once used in Scandinavia to curdle milk; indicator of base-poor wet ground.

lifecycle: Apr-May: tightly coiled fiddleheads unfurl. Jun-Aug: fronds fully expanded. Sep-Oct: fronds turn brown and die back. Winter: underground rhizomes dormant.
identification: Large triangular fronds emerging singly from the ground; often forms dense stands.
notes: Very widespread; can dominate vegetation; historically used for bedding, thatch and potash; contains compounds that can be carcinogenic to livestock if browsed heavily.

lifecycle: Evergreen; new fronds appear in spring. May-Aug: upright fertile fronds produced; spores ripen and shed through summer and autumn.
identification: Distinct two types of fronds: low spreading sterile fronds and taller narrow fertile fronds; dark green.
notes: Indicator of ancient woodland and acidic soils; adds structure to shaded banks and ravines.

lifecycle: Apr-Jun: new fronds unfurl. Summer: spores develop on underside. Late summer-autumn: spores shed; fronds die back.
identification: Robust fern with shuttlecock-shaped crown of fronds; sori (spore clusters) round on underside.
notes: Common woodland fern; roots were once used in remedies for intestinal worms (now obsolete due to toxicity and modern medicines).

habitat: Damp woodland, conifer plantations, shaded slopes
lifecycle: Similar to Male Fern: spring unfurling, summer spores, autumn die-back.
identification: Triangular, highly divided fronds with dark scales on midrib; forms dense crowns.
notes: Abundant in many Scottish woods, including conifer plantations; provides cover for small animals.

habitat: Moist woods, stream banks, shaded ravines
lifecycle: Apr-Jun: delicate fronds unfurl. Summer: spores develop and ripen. Autumn: fronds turn yellow/brown and die back.
identification: Graceful, finely divided fronds; stems often reddish at base; overall more delicate than Male Fern.
notes: Considered one of the prettiest native ferns; name may refer to its delicate appearance.

habitat: Acidic upland woods, stream sides, damp slopes
lifecycle: Spring: new fronds appear. Summer: spores develop on undersides. Autumn: fronds die back.
identification: Fronds smell faintly of lemon when crushed; pinnae with distinct marginal line of spore cases when fertile.
notes: Indicator of moist, acidic upland habitats; scent makes it distinctive in the field.

lifecycle: Jul-Aug: fruiting bodies begin to appear. Aug-Nov: peak fruiting. Winter-spring: only underground mycelium persists.
identification: Bright red cap with white warty spots; white gills and stem with ring; often near birch.
notes: Iconic 'fairy-tale' mushroom; highly toxic and psychoactive; associated with folklore and old shamanic practices.

habitat: Birch woodland and under solitary birches
lifecycle: Jul-Oct: fruiting bodies appear after rain; rest of year as mycelium linked to birch roots.
identification: Brown cap; stout stem covered in dark scales; always in association with birch trees.
notes: Edible when properly cooked and in good condition; classic birch-associated bolete.

lifecycle: Jul-Oct: fruiting period; otherwise underground.
identification: Orange to fox-red cap; pale stem with dark scabers; found with birch.
notes: Considered a good edible when well cooked; another mycorrhizal partner of birch.

lifecycle: Jul-Oct: yellow fruiting bodies appear, often in patches; dormant as mycelium for rest of year.
identification: Golden-yellow, funnel-shaped caps with forked ridges (false gills) on underside; fruity apricot smell.
notes: Highly prized edible mushroom; indicator of relatively undisturbed woodland; important to leave some behind for wildlife and spores.

habitat: On dead or dying birch trunks
lifecycle: Perennial bracket present year-round; new brackets appear mainly in late summer-autumn.
identification: Hoof-shaped to disc-like brackets; pale buff upper surface; always on birch.
notes: Used historically for sharpening blades and as tinder; Ötzi the Iceman carried it; plays key role in breaking down dead birch wood.

habitat: On dead hardwood logs and stumps
lifecycle: Fruiting brackets present most of year; growth strongest in warmer, wetter months.
identification: Thin, tough, multi-coloured bracket fungus with concentric zones like a turkey's tail.
notes: Well-known medicinal mushroom in some traditions; important wood-decaying species; very common.

habitat: On elder and other dead hardwood branches
lifecycle: Can fruit at almost any damp time, often autumn-spring.
identification: Brown, rubbery ear-shaped fungus growing on branches.
notes: Edible and used in some cuisines when properly identified; long history of medicinal use particularly in East Asia.

lifecycle: Aug-Oct: puffballs appear and mature; spores released in puffs when squeezed or trodden on.
identification: Small pear-shaped white to buff fungi with spiny warts; turn brown and form central hole when mature.
notes: Edible when young and white throughout; children often enjoy watching the 'smoke' of spores puff out when trodden on.

habitat: Pine and birch woodland floors, heaths
lifecycle: Evergreen; new 'steps' of growth added each year, most visible in spring and summer; spores in capsules when produced.
identification: Feathery moss with layered 'steps' along stem; forms soft mats.
notes: Characteristic moss of boreal and upland woods; provides shelter and moisture for invertebrates and seedlings.

lifecycle: Evergreen carpets; sporophytes occasionally produced, mostly in growing season.
identification: Feather-like shoots with distinctive reddish stems; forms thick carpets.
notes: Very common in conifer forests; important in nutrient and water retention in the forest floor.

habitat: Wet heaths, bog edges, woodland rides
lifecycle: Evergreen; sporophyte stalks with capsules produced mainly spring-summer.
identification: Tall, robust moss forming dense stands; star-like leaf arrangement at shoot tips; tall brown spore capsules on stalks.
notes: Large and conspicuous moss; contributes to structure of boggy and heathland ground layers.

lifecycle: Active year-round in wet conditions; growth and peat formation continuous; sporophytes produced sporadically.
identification: Soft, spongy, water-holding moss in many colours (green, red, yellow); forms hummocks and carpets in very wet places.
notes: Key peat-forming moss; can hold many times its weight in water; historically used as wound dressing and as absorbent material.

lifecycle: Present year-round; growth slow but continuous in suitable conditions.
identification: Pale grey branched lichen forming bushy mats resembling tiny leafless shrubs.
notes: Important winter food for reindeer in some regions (hence the name); very slow-growing; sensitive to trampling and pollution.

habitat: On twigs and branches of trees, especially in clean air
lifecycle: Ever-present; grows slowly; may produce fruiting bodies in mature colonies.
identification: Pale greenish fruticose lichen hanging in tufts like little beards from branches.
notes: Indicator of clean air; some Usnea species used traditionally as antiseptics; adds a 'mossy' character to trees.

lifecycle: Present year-round; slow-growing.
identification: Grey, leafy lichen forming rosettes with black undersides and pale lobes.
notes: Historically used as a source of purple-brown dye (croft dye); common in Scottish uplands.

habitat: On tree branches, especially in humid woods
lifecycle: Evergreen; growth slow; fruiting bodies infrequent.
identification: Short, bushy, pale green lichen with flattened fronds; aromatic when crushed.
notes: Used in perfumery (oakmoss absolute); indicator of clean, humid woodland air.

lifecycle: Evergreen mats; sporophytes occasional.
identification: Glossy green to yellow-green creeping moss forming dense carpets on soil or rock.
notes: Common on heather moorland; contributes to ground cover and moisture retention.

lifecycle: Evergreen; sporophytes occasional.
identification: Bright green moss with arching shoots and leaves that bend back, giving a 'starry' look.
notes: Very common in lawns and grasslands; resilient and tolerant of trampling.
